paper_title: Plans Don't Persist: Why Context Management Is Load Bearing for LLM Agents

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Long-horizon agents depend on context management: systems compress, summarize, and evict old tokens so tasks can continue beyond finite windows. That is safe only when dropped information is no longer needed or has been internalized. Plans are the stress case: they are written early, used for many steps, and first to be evicted. We introduce replay pairing, a diagnostic that runs the same trajectory with and without the plan in history and measures hidden-state cosine distance. On Llama-3.1-70B, plan signal spikes to 0.453 one step after the plan, then falls 4.1x in a single action-observation step; HotpotQA falls 12.4x. This is evidence that standard LLM agents do not carry plans forward as persistent state, and instead depend on the plan remaining in context. A layer-L32 probe detects this decay as a diagnostic, not as proof that it reads plan content itself. Reasoning models add a measurement confound: their `<think>` traces re-derive plan content, so standard stripping leaves plan evidence in the stripped condition. We name this the reasoning-trace confound and fix it with strict stripping, which removes prior `<think>` blocks from the stripped run only. It recovers +163% of the step+1 signal in-sample and +153% held out, while not meaningfully changing non-reasoning Llama (+4.8%). On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, a Llama-trained probe transfers at AUROC 0.748 (p=6e-4), while R1-specific probes reach 1.000, suggesting R1 encodes plan signal in a different hidden-state direction. Finally, a compression stress test shows the practical cost: naive plan eviction cuts ALFWorld success by 34.7pp, while probe-gated re-surfacing does not recover it. The contribution is a measurement and stress-test framework showing that agent-critical information can be context-resident rather than persistent. Context management is load bearing, but plan protection alone is not enough.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 2 2 ] . [ 1 3 5 9 2 2 . 6 0 6 2 : r Preprint. Plans Dont Persist: Why Context Management Is Load Bearing for LLM Agents Aman Mehta Snowflake AI Research aman.mehta@snowflake.com Anupam Datta Snowflake AI Research anupam.datta@snowflake.com

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Long-horizon agents depend on context management: systems compress, summarize, and evict old tokens so tasks can continue beyond finite windows. That is safe only when dropped information is no longer needed or has been internalized. Plans are the stress case: they are written early, used for many steps, and first to be evicted. We introduce replay pairing, diagnostic that runs the same trajectory with and without the plan in history and measures hidden-state cosine distance. On Llama-3.1-70B, plan signal spikes to 0.453 one step after the plan, then falls 4.1 in single action-observation step; HotpotQA falls 12.4. This is evidence that standard LLM agents do not carry plans forward as persistent state, and instead depend on the plan remaining in context. layer-L32 probe detects this decay as diagnostic, not as proof that it reads plan content itself. Reasoning models add measurement confound: their <think> traces re-derive plan content, so standard stripping leaves plan evidence in the stripped condition. We name this the reasoning-trace confound and fix it with strict stripping, which removes prior <think> blocks from the stripped run only. It recovers +163% of the step+1 signal in-sample and +153% held out, while not meaningfully changing non-reasoning Llama (+4.8%). On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, Llama-trained probe transfers at AUROC 0.748 (p=6104), while R1-specific probes reach 1.000, suggesting R1 encodes plan signal in different hidden-state direction. Finally, compression stress test shows the practical cost: naive plan eviction cuts ALFWorld success by 34.7pp, while probe-gated re-surfacing does not recover it. The contribution is measurement and stress-test framework showing that agent-critical information can be context-resident rather than persistent. Context management is load bearing, but plan protection alone is not enough.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Context management is becoming load-bearing for LLM agents. To run long horizons within finite window, deployments compress, summarize, and evict old context, betting that dropped tokens do not change behavior. Whether that bet is safe depends on where information lives: in context-time memory (text in the window, re-read each step) or in persistent internal state that survives once the text is gone. The agents plan is the sharpest case. It is written once, early, so it is first to be evicted. If the plan is held in internal state, dropping its text is free; if it is only re-read each step, dropping it silently breaks an agent that passed every behavioral test while the text was still visible. This did not matter when contexts were short and nothing scrolled away; modern context management makes it matter. Which mechanism holds is the question this paper answers. Planning frameworks like ReAct (Yao et al., 2022), Chain-of-Thought (Wei et al., 2022), Tree of Thoughts (Yao et al., 2023), and Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) all assume the same thing: the agent writes plan, and the plan guides what it does next. Behavioral alignment metrics confirm that it does. But behavior alone cannot tell two mechanisms apart. The plan could be held in hidden state across the trajectory (internalized), or it could live only as text in 1 Preprint. the window and be re-read each step (context-time). While the plan text stays visible, both mechanisms produce the same actions. They differ only once the text leaves the window, which is exactly where context compression, summarization, and KV-cache eviction operate. To separate the two, we use replay pairing (Figure 1). For each trajectory we run two matched conditions: keeps the plan in history; replays the same trajectory with the plan removed. The plan-fidelity signal is the cosine distance between the two hidden states at each step. It measures how much the hidden state changes when the plan is taken out of history. This signal also mixes in position, history length, and discourse structure (3); lengthand content-matched controls are the main follow-up (9). In Llama-3.1-70B ReAct agent on ALFWorld (Shridhar et al., 2020), the plan signal jumps to 0.453 at step+1, drops 4.1 in one action-observation cycle, and settles near 0.027 by step+5. HotpotQA drops even faster (12.4). Ridge probe at the peak layer (L32) predicts the signal at R2 = 0.875, separates active from decayed plans at AUROC 0.999, and transfers to HotpotQA with no retraining. One caveat: step index is also almost perfectly decodable here (R2=0.978 at L32), so part of the AUROC reflects trajectory phase, not plan content. We have not run the mixed-effects control that would separate the two (5). The headline reading holds either way: the plan-presence signal is context-window effect that fades within step, not state the agent keeps. Reasoning-trace contamination. Reasoning models emit <think>. . .</think> traces before each action (DeepSeek-AI, 2025). Run unchanged on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, replay pairing makes the step+1 signal look 4.5 smaller than Llamas (0.022 vs. 0.099), as if R1 barely encodes the plan. This is measurement artifact. The model re-states plan content inside prior <think> blocks, and standard stripping leaves those blocks in place, so both conditions still carry the plan and the distance under-counts. Strict stripping removes prior <think> blocks from condition only. It recovers +163% in-sample, +153% held-out, and changes Llamas signal by just +4.8%. The Llama-trained L32 probe on R1 strict-stripped states (n=19) reaches AUROC 0.748 (p=6104); R1 self-probes reach 1.000 on two distilled families, along direction 89.3 from Llamas. Scope. The claims are representational, not behavioral: strict stripping has small, nonsignificant task-success effects, and uniform L32 steering is null. Content-specific controls and stronger causal tests remain future work. Contributions. 1. Evidence that the plan is context-time object: in standard LLMs it decays 412 within one step out of hidden state, with cross-domain and within-family replication. 2. compression stress test: naive plan eviction cuts ALFWorld success by 34.7pp, and probe-gated re-surfacing does not recover it (8). 3. Replay pairing as measurement method, plus the reasoning-trace confound and strict stripping fix, which recovers +163% of step+1 signal on R1 and is verified no-op on non-reasoning Llama. 4. Probe-transfer and self-probe evidence that corrected hidden states carry planrelated signal, with R1 using different hidden-state direction from Llama.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Context-time vs. weight-time memory. growing line of work asks where information model uses actually resides: in context-time memory (text in the window, supplied at inference) or weight-time memory (absorbed into parameters). Retrieval-head analysis shows that long-context factuality is carried by small set of attention heads that copy from the window rather than from parameters (Wu et al., 2024); multi-turn studies find that models get lost when relevant content is distributed across turns instead of re-stated (Laban et al., 2025). Context compression, summarization, and KV-cache eviction all bet that some tokens can be dropped without changing behavior. Our plan-fidelity measurement is direct test of that bet for one important token type: it asks whether the plan is re-read 2 Preprint. from the window each step or held in durable state, and finds the former in standard LLMs. The plan has no weight-time backup, which places it squarely in the context-time regime, where eviction is unsafe. Planning and faithfulness in LLM agents. The standard paradigm interleaves reasoning and acting (Yao et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Yao et al., 2023; Shinn et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2022). These frameworks assume explicit plans improve behavior, which behavioral metrics confirm, but they do not test the mechanism by which the plan influences subsequent steps. Repeated-run studies show that multi-step agents can diverge early under identical inputs, and that consistency can amplify both correct and incorrect interpretations (Mehta, 2026a;b). Our work asks complementary question: whether the plan representation itself persists across those steps. We extend plan-faithfulness analysis to the representational level and find that high behavioral alignment can coexist with 12 representational decay. Hidden-state representations. LLM hidden states linearly encode many semantic properties (Burns et al., 2023; Marks & Tegmark, 2024; Zou et al., 2023). Circuit tracing (Ameisen et al., 2025) and the Tuned Lens (Belrose et al., 2023; nostalgebraist, 2020) look at the layer dimension within one forward pass. Replay pairing operates on different axis: the temporal dimension of multi-step agent behavior. The question is how plan information persists across forward passes. Interventions and reasoning-model probes. Activation steering (Turner et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023), inference-time intervention (Li et al., 2023), and self-refinement (Madaan et al., 2023) target hidden states or context. For reasoning models, several works probe R1 hidden states in single-turn settings (Liu et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025a; Chen et al., 2025); Wang et al. (2025b) report poor instruction-following inside traces and Wu et al. (2025) propose Thinking Intervention; Stolfo et al. (2025) apply activation differencing single-turn. The piece we add is identifying the contamination that hits paired-trajectory methods on reasoning models in multi-turn agent loops, and fix.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: 3.1 Agent Setup We run two classes of model as ReAct agents (Yao et al., 2022). The non-reasoning model is Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024) (primary), with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct for within-family replication. The reasoning model is DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B (DeepSeek-AI, 2025), Llama-3.1-70B base distilled from DeepSeek-R1 reasoning traces. It produces explicit <think>. . .</think> blocks before each action. We inject <think>n prefix at generation time so the reasoning trace always opens with the right tag (the Llama chat template does not emit the opening <think> on its own). The primary environment is ALFWorld (Shridhar et al., 2020), text-based household environment where the agent completes tasks by navigating rooms and manipulating objects through natural-language action space (go_to, pick_up, put, heat, cool, clean, examine). It spans six task types ranging from 3-4 to 6-8 plan steps. We use the text-only (non-visual) variant, following standard ReAct-on-ALFWorld baselines (Yao et al., 2022). The secondary environment is HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018). We collect 30 trajectory pairs using the same replay-pairing protocol (4) and the same model and prompt configuration, for direct cross-domain comparison. Models run on 8H200 GPUs with pipeline parallelism. Full hidden states (R8192) are captured at the last token position at every forward pass. 3.2 Plan Elicitation To study plan faithfulness we first need an explicit plan, so we inject guard message asking the agent for multi-step plan: 3 Preprint. Figure 1: Replay pairing. The same trajectory is run twice: keeps the plan in history; replays the identical actions and observations with the plan removed. The plan-fidelity signal is the per-step cosine distance between the two hidden states. It spikes one step after the plan and fades to near zero within step: plan re-read from context, not stored in hidden state. Before continuing, please state your complete plan for finishing this task. Describe each step in order: where you will go, what you will pick up, what actions you will perform, and where you will place the object. The agent replies with sequential plan, which then stays in history for the rest of the task. We inject at step 2 in ALFWorld (step 4 in HotpotQA): late enough that the agent has seen the task and initial context, early enough to leave long post-plan trajectory for measurement. 3.3 Replay Pairing For each agent run we create two matched conditions. Condition (plan-present). The agent runs normally with the guard at step 2; the plan enters history. We capture hidden states at every step and layer ℓ. (s,ℓ) Condition (plan-stripped replay). We replay As exact trajectory (feeding As observations and action outputs) but strip the guard exchange, and call the LLM on this plan-free history to produce . Bs output is discarded; As trajectory drives the conversation. (s,ℓ) Both conditions see the same action-observation sequence; they differ only in whether the plan exchange is in history. Plan-fidelity signal at step and layer ℓ is: PlanSignal(s,ℓ) = 1 cos (cid:16) (s,ℓ) , (s,ℓ) (cid:17) (1) where step indices are relative to the guard: step 0 is the guard step, step +1 is the next agent turn, and so on. Pre-plan steps (s < 0) act as validation baseline. and are identical before the guard fires, so the signal at steps 2 and 1 should be zero. In practice it is below 103 (Appendix A). What plan-fidelity signal does and does not measure. and differ in plan content, but also in history length, the relative positions of subsequent tokens, and discourse structure. The pre-plan baseline rules out replay-mechanics confounds but does not separate plan content from these. The strongest content-isolation controls (length-matched filler, shuffledplan placebos, paraphrase and ablation) are the priority next step (9); plan-fidelity signal is the cosine distance attributable to the plan exchanges presence, composite that upperbounds the content-specific component. 3.4 Strict Stripping for Reasoning Models Standard replay pairing assumes that any hidden-state difference between and reflects only the conditioning input, i.e. the plan. For reasoning models this fails. As the model works through each subsequent action it re-states parts of the plan, both inside its <think>. . .</think> block and, to lesser degree, in the retained Thought:/Action: text. When those turns are replayed verbatim into condition B, that re-stated plan content reap4 Preprint. pears even though the original plan exchange was stripped, so both conditions still encode the plan and the cosine distance under-counts the true plan-following signal. Strict stripping is small change to the protocol. In addition to removing the plan exchange from Bs history, also remove every complete <think>. . .</think> block from each prior assistant turn. Content after </think> (Thought: and Action: lines) is kept so that still sees the same external action-observation sequence as A. The protocol has two sanity checks, both tested in Section 7: on models that emit no <think> content it should be no-op, and on reasoning models the strict-stripped plan signal should exceed the standard plan signal. What strict-stripping controls do not yet establish. The operator is asymmetric (applied to only) and removes whole <think> blocks rather than only plan-relevant spans. The recovered signal could therefore reflect removal of plan-content contamination (our hypothesis), removal of generic reasoning-history content, or an asymmetry-induced shift in Bs local distribution. The Llama no-op (+4.8%; 7) rules out the generic-reasoning mechanism but not the asymmetry one. Three controls would settle it: strip both and B, replace blocks with length-matched neutral text, or redact only plan spans. These are listed in 9. 3.5 Probe and Task Partitioning To turn plan signal into detector we train Ridge regression probe on plan-present hidden states at the peak layer ℓ (Section 4), regressing the scalar plan-signal magnitude y(s) = PlanSignal(s,ℓ); threshold τ converts predictions to fire/no-fire decision. We use regression rather than binary classification because step index alone is trivially decodable from the residual stream (Appendix A). We collect plan-signal data from 80 ALFWorld tasks (50 train, 30 held out), 30 HotpotQA pairs, and 5+5 R1-Distill-70B ALFWorld tasks for the contamination analysis; all splits are disjoint (Appendix A).

sections.title: 4 Experiments: Plan Signal Decay
sections.content: 4.1 Overall Decay Curve Figure 3 shows plan signal vs. step offset from the guard on ALFWorld (80 tasks). Pre-plan steps are sanity check: and are identical before the guard, and we observe mean signal < 108 at steps 2, 1 (floating-point noise), confirming the replay pairing is correct. At step +1 plan signal jumps to 0.453 0.039, drops to 0.110 0.032 at step +2 (4.1 in one cycle), and settles to 0.027 by step +5. The initial magnitude nearly matches HotpotQA (0.453 vs. 0.445) though HotpotQA decays faster (12.4; 6). In context-management terms: the plans footprint in hidden state is nearly gone within one step, so anything downstream that needs the plan must read it back from the window. 4.2 Layer and Task-Type Profile Step +1 plan signal peaks sharply at layer 32 (signal 0.686 0.024), with L28/L36 forming broad mid-network peak; early and late layers are lower. We use L32 as ℓ. Stratified by task type, all six ALFWorld types show closely matched spikes (0.445-0.474) and decay rates (4.0-4.4): complex plans (6-8 steps) decay as fully as simple ones (3-4), so the dynamics are architectural, not task-level (Figure 2).

sections.title: 5 Detection: Probe for Plan Decay
sections.content: 5.1 Probe Performance We train Ridge regression probe on layer-ℓ hidden states from 80 ALFWorld tasks (1360 post-plan steps) using 5-fold stratified cross-validation. The probe regresses plan-signal magnitude. threshold τ converts predictions to binary fire/no-fire decision. Table 1 reports results at ℓ = 32 together with zero-shot transfer to HotpotQA. 5 Preprint. (a) Layer profile at step+1. (b) Decay by task type. Figure 2: Plan signal is localized in depth but stable across tasks. The peak layer is L32 (signal 0.686), and all six ALFWorld task types converge to the same residual. Figure 3: Plan signal (mean SE, 80 tasks) as function of step offset from guard injection. Pre-plan signal (s<0) is near zero (validation). Signal jumps to 0.453 at step +1 then drops 4.1 in one step. On ALFWorld, the probe predicts plan-signal magnitude at R2 = 0.875 0.016, well below the R2 = 1 trivially-leaked feature would produce. The induced binary decision at τ = 0.15 reaches AUROC 0.999 with balanced precision (0.985) and recall (0.825). Applying the ALFWorld probe direction zero-shot to HotpotQA gives AUROC 1.000 at both L32 and L40. Caveat: probe AUROC near 1.0 is partly step-index leakage. Step index is near-perfectly decodable from the residual stream (R2=0.978 at L32; Appendix A), and plan-signal magnitude is correlated with step index by construction, so probe that learns predict step index would also score high on the active-vs-decayed contrast. We have not run the discriminating control: mixed-effects analysis holding step index, task type, plan length, and observation-plan overlap fixed. Until then the AUROC should be read as at least as informative as step index, not as content-specific representation. The cross-domain transfer is more informative than the in-domain AUROC. At the ALFWorld cutoff τ = 0.15, L40 transfer is sharp on HotpotQA (F1 0.968); L32 keeps full recall but fires on HotpotQA-specific variance (precision 0.513), which short isotonic recalibra6 Preprint. Table 1: Probe performance. ALFWorld: Ridge regression at ℓ = 32, 5-fold CV on 1360 steps across 80 tasks. HotpotQA transfer: zero-shot application of the ALFWorld probe direction to 238 HotpotQA steps (60 positive, 178 negative) at two destination layers. R2 is reported for regression; binary metrics are computed by thresholding regression output at τ = 0.15. Metric ALFWorld HotpotQA L32 HotpotQA L40 R2 AUROC F1 (τ=0.15) Precision Recall 0.875 0.016 0.999 0.898 0.985 0. n/a 1.000 0.678 0.513 1.000 n/a 1.000 0.968 0.938 1.000 tion lifts to F1 0.920. Some component of the plan-presence direction generalizes across domains beyond what step index would predict, but the operating point needs per-domain calibration. 5.2 Probe Validity, Gradedness, and Calibration Three controls back the probe (full numbers in Appendix A): shuffled-label control and step-index regressor confirm the residual fit (R2 = 0.875) captures structure beyond the R2 = 0.978 step-index baseline at L32; 4-class step classifier attains macro F1 = 0.862 with strictly monotonic confusion; and the main binary probe is near-perfectly calibrated (Brier 106). 5.3 Lag Analysis: Early Warning The probe is useful only if it fires before behavioral failure. Defining lag = sdeviate sprobe_fire (deviation = first action with plan-alignment < 0.3 from Claude Opus 4 judge), of the 80 tasks 31 deviated, and on those the probe fires on average 4.45 steps ahead (median 5), leading deviation in 74.2% of cases. 4-5 step lead is well within ALFWorlds 20-step cap, so context manager could use the probe to decide when to re-surface the plan into the window.

sections.title: 6 Cross-Domain and Cross-Model Validation
sections.content: We test the standard-LLM result along two axes: domain (HotpotQA) and scale (Llama-3.18B). Cross-domain (HotpotQA). On 30 HotpotQA pairs with the same Llama-3.1-70B, step +1 plan signal is 0.445 and drops to 0.036 at step +2 (12.4, < 0.001); the initial magnitude nearly matches ALFWorld (0.453). The faster drop fits HotpotQAs observations being Wikipedia passages unrelated to the plan, vs. ALFWorlds plan-relevant room states. The peak layer is L34 (vs. L32); the encoding locus is domain-invariant. The ALFWorld-trained probe transfers zero-shot at AUROC 1.000, with isotonic recalibration lifting L32 transfer to F1 = 0.920. The direction is domain-invariant; only the operating point needs recalibration. Cross-scale (Llama-3.1-8B). On 20 ALFWorld tasks, plan signal peaks at 0.382 and decays 10.9 by step +5, matching the 70B shape; the peak sits at 37.5% relative depth (L12 of 32) vs. 40% (L32 of 80) for 70B, as the attention-reconstruction account predicts: plan signal lives in depth-relative band, not fixed layer (full multi-model panel in Appendix A.7). 7 Preprint.

sections.title: 7 Plan Maintenance in Reasoning Models
sections.content: In the standard-LLM regime, plans are not stored across steps; they are re-read from context. Reasoning models behave differently, and standard replay pairing does not see the difference. The apparent deficit. On 5-task ALFWorld subset, standard replay pairing on DeepSeekR1-Distill-Llama-70B (DeepSeek-AI, 2025) gives step +1 plan signal of 0.022, versus 0.099 for Llama-3.1-70B on the same tasks, so R1 looks 4.5 weaker. Taken at face value, reasoning model that emits explicit <think> traces appears to encode the plan less than standard instruct model. Three hypotheses, one cause. Dilution (a uniform R1/Llama ratio across depth) is ruled out: the ratio varies 4.4 across layers and the models peak at different layers (L72 vs. L32). Position mismatch is small (Llama exceeds R1 at every matched position). The remaining cause is contamination via re-derivation: re-stated plan content inside prior <think> blocks reappears in Bs history, so both conditions encode plan content (Appendix A). Strict-strip correction and verification. Re-running with strict stripping raises step +1 plan signal from 0.022 to 0.058, +163% change; every in-sample task moves the same way (Appendix A.5) and the R1-Llama gap shrinks from 4.5 to 1.7. Five heldout tasks of unseen types yield +153%. On non-reasoning Llama strict stripping is +4.8% no-op, ruling out generic reasoning-history mechanism. The recovery is large but partial, and the residual 1.7 gap is consistent with plan content also re-stated in the retained Thought:/Action: text, which strict stripping does not remove; redacting plan spans wherever they occur is the clean follow-up (9). Probe transfer. The Llama-trained L32 probe applied zero-shot to R1 strict-strip (n=19) reaches AUROC 0.748 (p=6104); R1 self-probes (n=85) reach 1.000, in direction 89.3 from Llamas L32  (Table 5)  . R1-Distill-Qwen-32B is the same (self-probe 1.000, n=85). On third family, Qwen3-32B-native (enable_thinking=True; n=15, 270 step pairs), the L26 self-probe fits per-step plan-fidelity at R2=0.997 but its binary spike-vs-decay AUROC is only 0.616: Qwen3-native shows persistent plan-conditional drift (cos-distance rises from 0.030 at step +2 to 0.13 by step +19) instead of spike-then-decay. So linear encoding of plan presence holds across all three reasoning families; the spike-decay timing is specific to distilled R1. Behavioral check (cross-family, n=25). Across R1-Distill-Llama-70B, Qwen3-32B-thinking (Qwen Team, 2025), and their non-reasoning baselines, behavioral effects are small and not significant: both reasoning models score 0/25 raw, and strict stripping moves Qwen3thinking to 2/25 (matching its no-thinking twin) while R1 stays 0/25 (Fisher p=0.49). Our claim is representational, not behavioral; it rests on the paired-trajectory and probe-transfer numbers above (full Phase-1 detail in Appendix B, Table 8).

sections.title: 8 When Context Management Fails: A Compression Stress Test
sections.content: The decay finding makes falsifiable operational prediction: if the plan is context-time object with no internal backup, compression policy that evicts plan tokens should sharply hurt task success, and the early-warning probe (5.3) should be able to re-surface the plan in time to prevent the loss. We test both on 30 ALFWorld tasks (5 runs = 150 runs/policy, Llama-3.1-70B, 20-step cap) under four context policies at matched budget (keep_recent=4 messages plus the system prompt): none (no eviction); naive (system prompt + last 4 messages); plan_protected (naive with the plan span always pinned); and probe_gated (naive, re-injecting the plan only on steps where the probe fires). Significance is paired permutation (10k) with bootstrap 95% CIs (Table 9 in Appendix C). Eviction is catastrophic; protecting the plan does not fix it. Naive eviction nearly thirds task success (56.7% 22.0%, 34.7pp, < 0.001): agents keep no usable plan once its tokens leave the window, exactly as the decay curve predicts. But neither plan-aware policy recovers (p=0.89 and 0.67 vs. naive), even though probe_gated re-surfaces the plan 6.1 8 Preprint. Figure 4: Context-compression stress test on 30 ALFWorld tasks and 5 runs per task. Naive eviction sharply reduces task success, while two plan-aware policies do not recover performance at the same context budget. Error bars show standard errors. times per run. The likely reason: at keep_recent=4 the evicted context is mostly recent observations and actions, the working state the agent needs, which re-surfacing stale plan does not restore. So the lesson is sharper than protect the plan: plan tokens are unsafe to drop, but plan-protection alone is not compression fix; plan-fidelity monitoring is diagnostic, not turnkey eviction policy.

sections.title: 9 Discussion and Limitations
sections.content: Implications for context management. The plan is case study in general question: which tokens an agent can safely drop. The same replay-pairing test applies to other tokens that may live only in context, such as safety instructions, constraints, and tool schemas. Reasoning models add second mechanism: their <think> traces re-derive plan content step by step, partially substituting for keeping the plan in the window (and why naive replay pairing under-counts on them). Since probe-gated re-surfacing did not recover the static-plan loss (8), whether such self-refreshing scratchpad survives compression better than static block is an open question we hope this workshop takes up. What we do not establish. The contribution is methodological and representational, not behavioral. Open controls remain: plan-content specificity vs. position/length/discourse; whether the orthogonal R1 direction is distinct concept or rotated encoding; strict stripping specificity; keep_recent budget sweep; probe causality beyond step index at scale (n100); and replication beyond our models and domains.

sections.title: A Implementation Details
sections.content: Models, decoding, infrastructure. Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024), Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B (DeepSeek-AI, 2025) served on 8H200 GPUs with pipeline parallelism. Decoding settings differ by experiment. The pairedtrajectory experiments (Sections 4 to 7, n=80 ALFWorld and n=30 HotpotQA) and the early-warning analysis use temperature 0.7, top-p 0.9, max-new-tokens 512, single run per task. The cross-model behavioral sweep (7) uses nruns=3 at T=0.7 for reasoning models and nruns=1 at T=0 for non-reasoning baselines. Deterministic decoding on nonreasoning baselines keeps variance out of the headline non-reasoning number; reasoning models are non-deterministic by default so we average over runs instead. The two settings are deliberate, and the non-reasoning Phase 1 condition is the only one with T=0. We use the ReAct (Yao et al., 2022) two-shot prompt template (pick_and_place_simple, pick_heat_then_place_in_recep). Hidden states are extracted at the last-token position; layer sampling stride 4 for layers 24-56 and stride 8 elsewhere (15 sampled layers for 70B, 9 for 8B). Compute budget. Wall-clock per experiment on 8H200 (70B-class) unless noted. Pairedtrajectory ALFWorld collection (n=80 tasks 2 conditions, single-pass replay with hiddenstate caching) takes about 36 H200-hours. HotpotQA cross-domain replay (n=30 pairs) takes about 8 H200-hours. R1-Distill-Llama strict-strip recovery (n=10 pairs 4 modes: raw, strict, in-sample, held-out) takes about 22 H200-hours. The Phase 1 cross-model behavioral sweep (6 conditions n=25 tasks, reasoning nruns=3) takes about 58 H200-hours total. R1-Distill-Qwen-32B and Qwen3-32B-thinking probe-transfer extensions (2H200 each, n=15-85 paired) take about 14 H200-hours combined. The steering sweep (n=150 paired observations, α=0.5, nruns=5 pooled) takes about 18 H200-hours. Probe training and analysis (Ridge or logistic regression on cached hidden states, no GPU) takes about 2 CPU-days. Total reported compute: about 156 H200-hours. Including preliminary and exploratory runs not in the paper (intervention sweeps that did not enter, extra collection on archival models, method-development sweeps over layer sampling and calibration), the full project consumed roughly 3 the reported total, about 450-500 H200-hours on shared internal cluster. Probe training. Logistic regression (binary, boundary, 4-class) with lbfgs solver, = 1.0, max_iter = 5000, 5-fold stratified cross-validation on L2-normalized features. Ridge regression with α = 1.0. Operating threshold τ = 0.15 chosen on validation folds to maximize F1. Validity controls (full numbers). At L8: real AUROC 1.000, shuffled 0.297 (gap 0.703), within-step 0.588, step-index R2 0.998. At L32: step-index R2 0.978. Top-10 feature dimensions at L8 and L32 are disjoint (IoU = 0.0). 11 Preprint. Graded probe per-class. 4-way classifier at L32, 5-fold CV, 80/class. Precision/recall/F1: step+1 0.988/1.000/0.994; step+2 0.876/0.888/0.882; step+3 0.728/0.738/0.733; step+5 0.857/0.825/0.841. Errors are strictly monotonic on adjacent decay classes. Cross-domain calibration. Isotonic regression fit on 10-task HotpotQA calibration split (93 steps), evaluated on 20 held-out tasks (145 steps); splits fixed and disjoint from ALFWorld training. Strict stripping (reasoning models). All complete <think>. . .</think> blocks are removed from each prior assistant turn in condition B. Content after </think> (Thought: and Action: lines) is kept so sees the same external action-observation sequence as A. The protocol is no-op (+4.8%, within noise) on Llama-3.1-70B, which emits no <think> content. Intervention harness. The intervention harness uses ALFWorld in standard DQN evaluation mode and reports task success via info["won"]. The probe is queried at the last-token position of each step. re-anchoring intervention fires when the predicted plan signal falls below τ = 0.10. worked example on pick_heat_then_place: the probe fires at step 5 (0.041 < τ), the re-anchoring intervention is applied, and the trajectory ends with won=True at step 7. Judge. Behavioral alignment scoring uses single LLM judge (Claude Opus 4 family) at temperature 0; alignment threshold for deviation set at 0.3 on the [0, 1] scale. Cross-model scope (negative result). Archival hidden states from Phi-3-Medium and Qwen2.5-72B were collected in single-run regime where step-level activations are bitidentical across re-runs of the same question. Plan signal is paired contrast, so it is undefined on those data, and we make no claim about non-Llama architectures from existing corpora. The most informative next experiments are replay-paired collection on mixture-of-experts model and on an RL-tuned reasoning model. Both need fresh data collection per architecture. A.1 Layer Profile and Task-Type Decay The layer profile and per-task-type decay curves are promoted to Figure 2 in the main text because they show the core robustness check: the peak is localized at L32, and all six ALFWorld task types show nearly identical decay dynamics regardless of plan length. A.2 Probe Validity Controls and Leakage Analysis Because plan signal is by construction maximal at step +1 and minimal at later steps, probe trained on this contrast risks learning which step is this rather than how much plan is present. Table 2 reports two controls: shuffled-label control (breaks the real relationship while keeping the step structure) and step-index regressor (predicts step index from the same hidden states). At L8, real binary AUROC is 1.000 but shuffled AUROC drops to 0.2971 and the step-index regressor reaches R2 = 0.998, so L8 representations carry nearly all step-index information for trivial classifier. At L32 the step-index R2 is still high (0.978), but the regression target (plan-signal magnitude) has graded structure (0.453, 0.110, 0.060, 0.027 at steps +1, +2, +3, +5) that step index alone does not predict. The probes residual fit (R2 = 0.875) is the non-trivial component. A.3 Graded Probe Confusion Matrix The 4-class probe ({+1, +2, +3, +5}) at L32, trained with 5-fold CV on 80 examples per class, attains overall accuracy 0.863, macro F1 = 0.862, macro AUROC 0.976  (Table 3)  . Confusion is monotonic: errors concentrate on adjacent steps, never on distant ones, consistent with one-dimensional plan-presence quantity that the probe orders correctly. 1Below 0.5 reflects small-sample 5-fold CV variance under shuffling; the relevant comparison is the drop from 1.000. 12 Preprint. Table 2: Probe validity controls. Real: binary AUROC of step+1 vs. step +3. Shuffled: same probe with labels permuted. Step R2: linear regressor predicting step index from hidden state. Layer Real AUROC Shuffled AUROC L8 L32 1.000 1.000 0.297 n/a Step R2 0.703 n/a 0.998 0.978 Figure 5: Step-index leakage at L8. Real binary AUROC (1.000) drops to chance (0.297) under label shuffling. The Ridge regression target at L32 (continuous plan-signal magnitude) sidesteps this confound. A.4 Probe Calibration Brier score and 10-bin Expected Calibration Error (ECE) at L32 are in Figure 6. The main binary probe (step +1 vs. +3) is near-perfectly calibrated (Brier = 2.5 106, ECE = 0.0002), which is unsurprising given the wide plan-signal gap between classes. The harder boundary probe (step +2 vs. +3, plan-signal gap 0.110 vs. 0.060) shows moderate miscalibration (ECE = 0.066), addressed via per-domain isotonic recalibration. Figure 6: Reliability diagram for the L32 probe. Diagonal indicates perfect calibration. A.5 Strict-Strip Per-Task Recovery Per-task strict-strip recovery on R1-Distill-Llama-70B is in Table 4. Every task shows positive recovery; the cross-task mean is +0.036 (+163%). Five held-out tasks covering two task types not in the in-sample set yield +153%. 13 Preprint. Table 3: 4-way graded probe at L32 (5-fold CV, 80 examples/class). Rows are true classes, columns predicted. True Pred +1 +2 +3 +5 +1 +2 +3 + 80 0 0 1 0 71 10 0 0 9 59 13 0 0 11 66 F1 0.994 0.882 0.733 0.841 Table 4: Strict-strip recovery on R1-Distill-Llama-70B (5 in-sample ALFWorld tasks). Task (type) Standard Strict-strip task_000 (pick_heat) task_002 (pick_two_obj) task_003 (pick_clean) task_004 (pick_and_place) task_006 (pick_and_place) Mean 0.029 0.020 0.020 0.018 0. 0.022 0.067 0.062 0.053 0.054 0.055 0.058 +0.038 +0.042 +0.033 +0.036 +0.032 +0. A.6 Reasoning-Model Per-Layer Plan Signal (Dilution Test) Table 5: Cross-regime probe transfer (referenced in 7). The Llama-trained L32 probe is at ceiling on Llama, drops on R1 raw, and partially recovers under strict stripping (p=6104, n=19). Self-probes reach ceiling on both R1 distillations. Qwen3-32B-native (enable_thinking=True) shows linear encoding (R2=0.997) without spike-decay locality (binary AUROC 0.616). The R1 self-probe is at 89.3 from Llamas L32 (cosine 0.012); R1Qwen = R1-Distill-Qwen-32B. Probe Eval target AUROC Llama ALFWorld Llama HotpotQA R1 raw (transfer) R1 strict (transfer) R1 strict Llama L32 Llama L32 Llama L32 Llama L32 R1 L32 (self) R1-Qwen L32 (self) R1-Qwen strict Qwen3-think Qwen3 L26 (self) enable_thinking=True, 270 step pairs. Regression R2=0.997. 0.999 1.000 0.600 0.748 [0.62,0.87] 1.000 1.000 0.616 80 25 5 19 85 85 15 To test the dilution hypothesis (that reasoning model spreads representation more thinly across layers, predicting uniform R1/Llama signal ratio across depth), we report per-layer plan signal at step +1 on the 5-task ALFWorld subset in Table 6. The R1/Llama ratio varies 4.4 across depth and the two models peak at different absolute layers (L72 for R1, L32 for Llama). Strong-form dilution is ruled out. A.7 Cross-Scale Multi-Model Panel Table 7 collects the cross-model decay results referenced in Section 6; Figure 7 summarizes them graphically. A.8 Intervention Sweeps and Head-Level Analysis Context-level re-anchoring. On 30 held-out ALFWorld tasks (max_steps = 200), the control, probe-triggered, and oracle always-replan conditions all land within 1 task of one another. This is clean 30-task null, which is what the in-context retrieval account predicts. 14 Preprint. Table 6: Per-layer step+1 plan signal (5 ALFWorld tasks). The R1/Llama ratio is far from uniform; peaks differ. Layer R1-Distill-70B Llama-70B Ratio L8 L32 L40 L48 L56 L72 L80 0.002 0.023 0.026 0.030 0.032 0.039 0.022 0.031 0.184 0.129 0.113 0.096 0.070 0. 0.07 0.12 0.20 0.27 0.33 0.56 0.50 Figure 7: Multi-model panel. Llama-3.1-70B: full replay-paired decay curve, peak L32, decay 4.1. Llama-3.1-8B: same shape at smaller scale (peak 0.382, 10.9 decay) at matched relative depth around 33%. Phi-3 and Qwen2.5: plan signal not computable from archival data (non-replay-paired). Residual-stream steering (nruns=5 paired, n=150 pooled). We sweep at L32 with α {0.5, 1.0, 2.0} on 30 held-out ALFWorld tasks 5 paired runs. At α=0.5 the pooled paired contrast is clean directional null: = + 1.33 pp (SR 54.7% 56.0%; paired Wilcoxon p=0.97; McNemar 11/12/7 help/hurt/tie, p=1.00; 95% bootstrap CI [8.0, +11.3]). Withinreplicate straddle zero: +8.9 pp at nruns=3, 10.0 pp at the new nruns=2 replicate. Pooling resolves them. α=1.0 and α=2.0 both yield 3.3 pp drops below baseline. Uniform residual addition along the L32 plan direction is not behavioral knob at this scale, which is the directional null the population-coded view predicts: single-axis push along measurement of the plan-presence contrast does not function as an intervention. Head-level structure. deterministic head-level analysis shows the plan direction is distributed across the 64 heads at L32 with mixed-sign top-3: top-3 captures 23% of ch, top-10 captures 55%. head-aware intervention that respects the signs of ch (n=90 paired, β=1.0) gives +3.3 pp at effective magnitude 0.094 (directional but within noise). At β=2.0 (effective magnitude 0.188) it flips to 4.4 pp. Scaling head-aware to match α=0.5s effective magnitude (β 5.3) destabilizes generation: the model stops emitting termination tokens within the LLM-call budget. Uniform addition at the same effective magnitude ran cleanly but landed at the n=150 null above. Both intervention shapes (uniform at matched magnitude, head-aware at low magnitude) fail to reliably move task success. The asymmetry is what isolates the population-coded contrast as measurement of which heads carry productive vs. antagonistic plan signal, rather than as knob that can be scaled arbitrarily. A.9 Why Does ALFWorld Decay Slower than HotpotQA? The 3 gap between ALFWorld decay (4.1) and HotpotQA decay (12.4), given the near-identical initial signal (0.453 vs. 0.445), has plausible structural explanation in the observations themselves. ALFWorld observations (You arrive at countertop 1. On the 15 Preprint. Table 7: Cross-model plan signal. Llama-3.1-70B: 80 ALFWorld tasks. Llama-3.1-8B: 20 tasks. Phi-3 / Qwen2.5: not replay-paired (archival-only data; plan signal undefined). Model Step+1 Residual Decay Peak layer Llama-3.1-70B Llama-3.1-8B Phi-3-Medium Qwen2.5-72B 0.453 0.382 4.1 10.9 L12/32 (37%) L32 (40%) 0.027 0.035 N/A (not replay-paired) N/A (not replay-paired) countertop 1, you see tomato 1.) are directly relevant to the plan (go to countertop, pick up tomato, heat it), so plan-relevant features stay in the residual stream across steps. HotpotQA observations (Wikipedia passages about unrelated entities) are informationally orthogonal to the plan (search for X, retrieve Y), which allows faster representational overwrite. If this account is right, plan-decay rate should correlate with the semantic overlap between plan content and observation content. This is testable hypothesis for future work. Phase 1 Cross-Condition Plan-Signal Post-Processing The Phase 1 cross-model sweep (Section 7) uses an independent-trajectory architecture. Each of the six conditions (r1_raw, r1_strict, qwen3_think_raw, qwen3_think_strict, llama_70b, qwen3_nothink) runs its own forward agent loop on each task (nruns = 3 for reasoning conditions, nruns = 1 for non-reasoning baselines). The behavioral task-success results are in Table 8. The 5+5 paired-trajectory replay-pair semantics from Section 7, in which condition replays the actions and observations of condition token-for-token, do not port directly to Phase 1, because no two Phase 1 conditions are paired by construction. Table 8: Phase 1 behavioral task success (n=25 ALFWorld tasks). Strict stripping moves Qwen3-thinking from 0% to 8%, matching its no-thinking baseline, but does not move R1-Distill-Llama-70B. Condition Solved Task SR Non-reasoning baselines llama_70b qwen3_nothink Qwen3-thinking family qwen3_think_raw qwen3_think_strict R1-Distill-Llama family r1_raw r1_strict 3/25 2/25 0/25 2/25 0/25 0/ 12.0% 8.0% 0.0% 8.0% 0.0% 0.0% To compute strict-strip recovery on Phase 1 data we use cross-condition post-processor. For each (task, r, t) with matching run index and step t, we load the saved hidden-state arrays from the matched raw and strict directories (r1_raw/task_NNN/run_RR/hs_step_TT.npy vs. r1_strict/task_NNN/run_RR/hs_step_TT.npy) and compute per-layer cosine distance: (task,r,t,ℓ) PlanSignal 1 = 1 cos (cid:16) (task,r,t,ℓ), hstrict hraw (task,r,t,ℓ) (cid:17) . (2) This measures the same conceptual quantity as the paired-trajectory plan signal (the representational change attributable to whether the contamination correction is applied), at the cost that the two trajectories are not action-replay-paired. We restrict comparisons to (task, r, t) cells where both the raw and strict trajectories executed the same step count without environment truncation, and report cross-task mean signal only over those cells. Within-model strict-vs-raw recovery is evaluated with paired permutation tests across the matched per-task means. The paired-trajectory +163% headline number is unaffected by this post-processor and remains the principal evidence for the methodological claim. 16 Preprint. Behavioral results do not depend on this post-processor. The Phase 1 task-success table  (Table 8)  uses only maxt score(t) > 0 counts per condition, which are computed directly from the saved trajectory results without any cross-condition pairing. The per-run success flag in our raw logs is decoupled from goal completion (it triggers on step-limit truncation), which is why we explicitly redefine real success as max -score > 0 throughout Section 7. Context-Compression Stress Test Details We evaluate four context policies on 30 ALFWorld tasks (task indices 50-79), 5 runs each (150 runs/policy), with Llama-3.1-70B as ReAct agent under 20-step cap. All policies share the budget keep_recent=4 retained messages plus the system prompt; the agents full message list is preserved internally, but only the compressed view is sent to the model on each call. The probe used by probe_gated is the L32 Ridge probe of 5, firing at threshold τ=0.15; on fire it re-injects the pinned plan span for that step. Per-policy success is maxt score(t) > 0. Comparisons use paired permutation test (10,000 permutations) with 95% bootstrap CIs over the 30 tasks; all results integrity-checked for 30 tasks 4 policies 5 runs with no errored or zero-step runs  (Table 9)  . Table 9: Context-compression stress test (150 runs/policy). and are paired permutation tests vs. the noted baseline; CIs are bootstrap 95%. Naive eviction is catastrophic (p < 0.001); neither plan-aware policy recovers (both CIs cross zero). Policy Success SE (ref) Resurf. none naive plan_protected probe_gated 56.7% 4.0% 22.0% 20.7% 24.7% n/a n/a 3.4% 34.7 (none) [44.7, 24.7] < 0.001 3.3% 1.3 (naive) [10.7, +8.0] 3.5% +2.7 (naive) [6.7, +12.0] 0.89 0.67 0.0 0.0 0.0 6.
